ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for stir-fried long beans with meat; mapped to the nearest stir-fried vegetable dish class. FoodOn does not have a specific term for yard-long bean stir-fry with meat as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vegetables, cooked — stir-fried mixed vegetable dish with meat
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for stir-fried long beans with meat; A0FJP (cooked vegetables) used as the closest defensible anchor. Meat component noted in description.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of stir-fried long beans with meat as a side dish at a Singapore hawker centre or home meal is approximately 150 g, representing one shared side portion alongside rice.

nutrient_source.title: Stir Fried Long Beans with Meat — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 'Stir Fried Long Beans with Meat' (cr_id F14080051). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this entry at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for a comparable stir-fried long bean and pork dish where HPB does not report disaggregated fat fractions.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entry for green beans / long beans (cooked vegetables, low-GI category)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for stir-fried long beans with meat as a composite dish. GI estimated at 20 (low) based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked green beans / yard-long beans (GI ~15–25, low category). The meat and oil components do not raise GI. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (20 × 5.04) / 100 ≈ 1.0. This is a conservative low-GI estimate consistent with published values for cooked leguminous vegetables.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 266 mg/100 g from soy sauce and oyster sauce; borderline for hypertension management
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should request reduced soy sauce and oyster sauce when ordering; a 150 g serving contributes approximately 399 mg sodium, which is meaningful within a 1500–2000 mg daily sodium target, and should be balanced against other low-sodium dishes in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate (3.36 g/100 g), zero sugar, low GI (~20), and 2.85 g/100 g dietary fibre slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Stir-fried long beans with meat is well-suited for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the low carbohydrate content, zero sugar, low glycemic index, and meaningful fibre from long beans collectively support postprandial glycaemic stability, and a 150 g serving contributes only approximately 5 g of carbohydrate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat approximately 2.1 g/100 g (estimated) and sodium 266 mg/100 g; fat profile depends on meat type used
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose chicken or lean pork over fatty pork cuts to reduce saturated fat intake, and request reduced soy sauce to lower sodium; the long beans contribute dietary fibre (2.85 g/100 g) which supports LDL cholesterol management, making this dish acceptable in a heart-healthy diet when prepared with lean meat.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid in red meat (pork); anti-inflammatory fibre and vitamin C from long beans provide partial offset
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: If pork is the meat used, individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should substitute with chicken or tofu to avoid arachidonic acid-driven amplification of inflammatory cytokine pathways; if chicken is confirmed, this dish is suitable for RA management.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Stir-Fried Long Beans with Meat (炒长豆角肉; Kacang Panjang Goreng dengan Daging) is a common Singaporean and Southeast Asian home-cooked and hawker dish consisting of yard-long beans (also called snake beans or long beans) stir-fried at high heat with sliced or minced meat — typically pork or chicken — together with aromatics such as garlic, and seasoned with soy sauce and oyster sauce. The dish is cooked quickly in a wok with oil, producing tender-crisp beans with a savoury, slightly caramelised flavour and a light coating of sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides 107 kcal, 6.0 g protein, 7.7 g fat, 3.36 g carbohydrate, 0 g sugar, 2.85 g dietary fibre, and 266 mg sodium, as reported by the HPB Singapore Foodo database. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 20 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is low in carbohydrates and sugar, making it appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. Sodium content is borderline for hypertension and should be moderated by reducing soy sauce and oyster sauce. The fat profile depends on the meat used; lean chicken reduces saturated fat compared to pork. Long beans are a good source of dietary fibre and vitamin C, supporting gut health and immune function. This dish is a staple side dish served alongside steamed rice in Singaporean hawker centres, coffee shops, and home kitchens, and is widely available across Chinese, Malay, and Peranakan food stalls.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14080051) at confidence 0.95. This is an exact match to an approved primary regional source. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy for a comparable dish; potassium estimated from USDA FoodData Central for cooked long beans. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked yard-long beans. Overall confidence is high given the exact HPB source match for all primary macros.
notes: sugar_g is confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14080051; long beans contain negligible free sugars and no added sugar is used in the standard preparation. Confirmed zero per HPB source. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: disaggregated fatty acid fractions, micronutrient detail, and moisture data for this composite stir-fried dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry F14080051, and no equivalent composite entry was found in USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. saturated_fat_g (2.1 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for stir-fried pork with vegetables as the closest approved-source analogue; this value should be updated when HPB publishes disaggregated fat data. potassium_mg (220 mg/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked yard-long beans combined with lean pork, used as a reasonable approximation. Dietary tags 'vegetarian' and 'plant_based' from the input record have been removed as this dish contains meat. 'keto' tag retained as the dish is very low in carbohydrates (3.36 g/100 g). Halal status is not confirmed as pork may be used; is_halal set to false pending confirmation of meat type. GI estimated at 20 (low) based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for cooked yard-long beans; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources.
